pitch: A fast 3D orbital tower defense where you protect the Sun from waves of cosmic enemies using planets, upgrades and tactical positioning.
inspiration:
I wanted to create a fast, instantly playable 3D tower defense that removes friction and focuses on clarity and pressure. The idea came from combining classic tower defense mechanics with a more dynamic orbital system, where the player defends the Sun using planets as strategic elements.

I was also inspired by the idea of “vibe coding”: using AI to rapidly iterate on gameplay, visuals, and systems, allowing me to focus more on design decisions and player experience rather than low-level implementation.

The goal was to build something simple to understand in seconds, but with enough depth to keep players engaged through increasing difficulty and strategic choices.

pitch: In 20 blind rounds, strategically allocate your workforce across the AI supply chain, decode hidden market signals, and outsmart 5 cunning AI rivals to dominate the industry.
inspiration:
Over the past few years, I've seen numerous reports about the artificial intelligence industry—opportunities and risks coexist, time is of the essence, companies make high-risk decisions with incomplete information, everyone is vying for scarce computing resources and data, and there's a constant tension between short-term gains and long-term consequences.

This is the inspiration behind my game. I wanted to transform this chaotic, stressful, yet absurdly captivating reality into a compact and easy-to-play gaming experience—a 20-round blind-play synchronous strategy game where players must allocate manpower throughout the AI ​​supply chain, interpret hidden market signals through tensions and news, cope with the shortage of computing resources, and strive for ultimate victory.

This theme feels particularly relevant today, and I hope it not only prompts reflection on the world we've collectively built but also provides entertainment in a fun and relaxing way.

pitch: 2D Street-style 7v7 arcade football.
inspiration:
I made this because I miss fun football games. I grew up playing NFL Street, and nothing today really captures that same fast, arcade-style energy. There haven’t been any real remakes or spiritual successors that hit that feeling.

At the same time, modern games like Madden NFL feel overly complex and hard to just pick up and play—I tried recently and couldn’t even easily get into a game.

Gridiron Rumble is my tribute to that older style—something simple, chaotic, and fun that you can jump into right away.

Note: there’s a brief initial load screen while the game loads assets for web. I kept the current setup to support cross-platform (Web / iOS / Steam) within the jam timeline.

pitch: A fast-paced 3D multiplayer cyberpunk arena where players fight, capture flags, and survive intense 5-minute battles in a neon dystopian city.
genre: Action, Shooter, Strategy, Multiplayer / PvP / Third-Person Shooter
inspiration:
I was inspired by fast-paced competitive games and the idea of building a fully playable multiplayer experience in a very short time using AI.

I wanted to explore how far AI-assisted development could go in creating not just a prototype, but a complete and responsive 3D multiplayer game. The cyberpunk setting came from my interest in futuristic dystopian worlds, combined with the need for a visually striking environment that could be built quickly and still feel immersive.

The goal was to create something simple, intense, and replayable — a game where players can jump in instantly, compete, and feel the action without complexity or barriers.

pitch: Play as a Developer and help people in your Neighborhood fixing their bugs in this 2D endless runner game.
inspiration:
The idea for the game was inspired by *Informatici di Quartiere*, an Italian non-profit association that helps people in local communities face everyday digital challenges through technology education, workshops, and even educational video games.

In the game, the player steps into the role of a developer who supports people in their neighborhood by solving tech-related problems and guiding people through the digital world. With a playful tone, the game tells the story of the association’s work while also making fun of classic programmer stereotypes — from being expected to fix everything, even printers, to facing the inevitable enemy of every developer: cable management.

inspiration:
BoneCrawler is my third small game project and my first public release since finishing school last year. Now 36, it reflects over 20 years immersed in computing and IT, beginning at age 12, shaped by my background as an IT specialist working across web development, networking, and systems architecture/administration. I’ve also spent years working with open-source and freeware game engines and editors since my teens.

This project was developed using AI-assisted workflows, where AI handled the code generation while I maintained the agentic workflow to keep the project aligned with its scope — building the game as an engine, rather than "just a game".

The primary stack included Claude, ChatGPT, and Codex for code generation and refinement, with Gemini used for planning and problem-solving. Development followed an iterative process: specifying systems, reviewing outputs, and refining results. AI produced the code, while I guided structure, tested, and ensured each component functioned as intended.

Development followed an Agile-style process adapted to this workflow. Different agents handled different layers of the project—game engine systems, UI/web components, and tooling—allowing parallel progress while maintaining a modular architecture. Version 1 released on April 1st; and a few days later, a functional engine editor and a complete rough build were in place. Since then, the project has been in an ongoing polish phase, with consistent updates pushed publicly on my GitHub to document progress.

The result is a modular, reusable engine built with AI-generated code (Vids of editor on my Youtube. WIP.). It is designed for extensibility, with plans to expand into a more advanced editor toolset that can support future projects. Once BoneCrawler has been live for a period, the engine will be open-sourced to share both the tools and the development approach. 

From a design perspective, BoneCrawler is a tribute to the games that influenced me most. It blends exploration inspired by The Legend of Zelda with faster-paced combat influenced by Devil May Cry/Ninja Gaiden, all within a dark, gothic fantasy setting influenced by The Lord of the Rings. Thank you!

pitch: Explore the galaxy, seed humanity's future, and read the stories of the colonies you leave behind.
inspiration: I wanted to build a game that made you feel the weight of leaving people behind. Most space games let you control everything. I wanted one where you make a decision, fly away, and come back a hundred years later to find out if the people you left actually made it. The AI-generated transmission system grew out of that. I didn't just want to show you numbers, I wanted you to read the last words of a colonist who knew the end was coming, or the joy of a settlement that beat the odds. The emotional core of the game is that you're responsible for these people but powerless to protect them. That tension is what drives everything.

pitch: Slide ancient hieroglyph stones across a sacred 5×5 grid, decipher the sequence carved in the shrine above, and unlock the wisdom the ancients buried for those worthy enough to find it.
inspiration:
I wanted to build something that felt like it had weight to it not just mechanically, but emotionally. The sliding puzzle mechanic is simple, but I layered it inside an Egyptian temple world because I was drawn to how the ancient Egyptians thought about the soul the idea that what you carry, what you put down, and what survives says everything about who you are.
The treasure you unlock at the end isn't gold or points. It's five chapters of text that speak directly to the player about resilience, identity, and what you're capable of. I wanted the game to feel like it was built for the person playing it.
The hieroglyphs, the stone, the music, the torchlight all of it was in service of one question: what if a puzzle game made you feel something real when you finished it?

game_name: Dr. Frogsworth and the Stolen Formulas
pitch: A retro pixel-art platformer (Mario × Mega Man homage) where Dr. Frogsworth blasts and hops through 5 realms of internet decay to liberate the meme formulas from the patent-hoarding Wojak King and Dr. Croak to keep the internet funny and free.
inspiration: Growing up on games like Super Mario Bros. and Mega Man 2, and watching modern meme culture get eaten by corporate patent-hoarding and algorithmic slop. I wanted a retro side-scroller where the villains are the ones trying to *prevent* memes from being freely stolen and remixed — because in classic meme culture, stealing = sharing = evolution. Dr. Frogsworth is the hero who made the formulas to be spread forever; the Wojak King and Dr. Croak are trying to gate keep them. It's a love letter to NES-era platformers and the early, free internet.

I wanted something engaging and original, so I thought bees vs wasps would be cool. Above all, something that's intuitive and that you can jump into right away, and that will keep you playing. As you go up levels stuff gets added, you get honeycomb, extra lives, and then more lethal wasps come in (Tarantula Hawk and Executioner at level 12+15), which was inspired by Brave Wilderness getting stung by them on YouTube. pitch: Grab a gun, shoot others and last stand wins.
inspiration:
Hello! I'm a UEFN & Unity developer with 6 years of experience. I know these engines well, but I'd never built a Three.js game before. I saw the game jam and thought, it's a good opportunity for creating project on ThreeJS.
The game itself is actually based on one of my UEFN maps. I had a lot of fun playing it in Fortnite, so I figured it'd be fun for others too, and it felt like a solid fit for a minimal jam project.
1.5 days for vibe-coding the game, server, and bug fixes, then 1.5 days for polish and deployment. 3 days total for a fully finished game. Wild man!!.
Honestly I don't think I could have shipped something like this in Unity in 3 days, especially with multiplayer. Three.js is amazing and also SUPER OPTIMIZED for PHONES! even for my 6 years old Galaxy Note10Lite.
Thanks for putting this jam together. It really pushed me to try something new.

I'm a big fan of games that you can play with friends to wind down after a long competitive gaming session. You just hop on in call and go play. That's what inspired me, creating a game that lets you breath and get some laughs in.

pitch: A vertical space shooter rendered entirely in ASCII text on HTML5 Canvas, inspired by Scramble (1981)
inspiration:
As a kid, I used to spend hours playing my uncle's TomyTronic Scramble — a handheld console game from 1981 that was ahead of its time. Just a tiny VFD screen and a game that kept pulling you back for one more run.
I wanted to build something inspired by that experience. Not a remake, but a game built around the same idea — that you don't need fancy assets or a game engine to make something enjoyable. You need the right mechanics.
